top_papers.title: Making Every Verified Token Count: Adaptive Verification for MoE Speculative Decoding
top_papers.summary: Tree-based speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by verifying multiple draft candidates in parallel, but this advantage weakens for sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models. As the draft tree grows, different branches activate different experts, expanding the union of activated experts and substantially increasing target-side verification cost. We propose EVICT, a training-free, hyperparameter-free, and lossless adaptive verification method for MoE speculative decoding. EVICT makes every verified token count by truncating the draft tree before target verification and retaining only the cost-effective prefix. It leverages fine-grained drafter signals to estimate candidate benefit, combines them with offline-profiled verification cost, and remains highly compatible with the high-performance graph-based serving framework SGLang. Extensive experiments on diverse MoE backbones and benchmarks show that EVICT achieves up to 2.35x speedup over autoregressive decoding and an average 1.21x speedup over the state-of-the-art baseline EAGLE-3, while significantly reducing unnecessary expert activations during verification.

ai.one_liner_zh: EVICT adapts MoE speculative-decoding verification by pruning draft-tree prefixes whose expected token benefit does not justify target-side expert activation cost.
ai.summary_zh: Tree-based speculative decoding can lose efficiency on sparse MoE models because wider draft trees make different branches activate different experts, raising target-side verification cost. EVICT is a training-free, hyperparameter-free, lossless method that truncates the draft tree before verification and keeps only the prefix estimated to be cost-effective. The estimate combines fine-grained drafter signals with offline-profiled verification cost, and the abstract says the method is compatible with SGLang. The paper reports experiments across diverse MoE backbones and benchmarks, with up to 2.35x speedup over autoregressive decoding and 1.21x average speedup over EAGLE-3, plus fewer unnecessary expert activations. The biggest missing details are the hardware and serving setup, the exact profiling procedure, and how robust the cost model is across workloads.
ai.background_zh: MoE LLM serving makes speculative decoding harder because verification cost depends on the union of experts activated by draft branches, not just token count.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks how to preserve speculative decoding gains for MoE models when larger draft trees inflate target-side expert computation.
ai.approach_zh: EVICT scores candidate draft prefixes using drafter-side benefit signals and offline verification-cost profiles, then truncates the tree before target verification.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports extensive experiments on diverse MoE backbones and benchmarks, claiming up to 2.35x speedup over autoregressive decoding, 1.21x average speedup over EAGLE-3, and reduced unnecessary expert activations; it does not specify hardware, batch settings, or baseline implementation details.
ai.value_zh: If the claims hold, this is a practical serving-side optimization for MoE speculative decoding that targets wasted expert activations without retraining.
open_questions: How are the offline verification-cost profiles collected, and do they remain valid across hardware, batch size, sequence length, and routing distributions?
What exact drafter signals are used to estimate prefix benefit, and what runtime overhead do they add?
How fair is the EAGLE-3 comparison in terms of implementation, SGLang integration, batching policy, and MoE backbone configuration?
core_contributions: Identifies expert-union growth across speculative draft branches as a MoE-specific verification bottleneck.
Introduces adaptive pre-verification tree truncation that aims to keep only cost-effective draft prefixes.
Combines drafter-derived benefit estimates with offline-profiled target verification costs without training or hyperparameter tuning, according to the abstract.
why_read: Directly targets LLM inference serving efficiency for sparse MoE models.
Relevant to speculative decoding systems where verification cost, not draft generation alone, determines throughput.
Includes claimed integration compatibility with SGLang, making it potentially useful for production-oriented serving work.
risks: The abstract does not disclose hardware, batch sizes, sequence lengths, or request mix, which are central to serving-speed claims.
Offline cost profiling may be sensitive to deployment configuration, but the abstract does not establish portability across systems.
recommended_for: LLM serving researchers working on speculative decoding
Engineers optimizing MoE inference throughput and expert activation cost
Systems readers tracking SGLang-compatible decoding optimizations
ai.reading_priority_reason: High priority because it directly matches the LLM inference systems focus, targets a concrete MoE serving bottleneck, and reports speedups over both autoregressive decoding and EAGLE-3, though the full read must verify evaluation setup and cost-model robustness.
ai.fallback_summary: Tree-based speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by verifying multiple draft candidates in parallel, but this advantage weakens for sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models.

top_papers.title: Silicon Showdown: Performance, Efficiency, and Ecosystem Barriers in Consumer-Grade LLM Inference
top_papers.summary: The operational landscape of local Large Language Model (LLM) inference has shifted from lightweight models to datacenter-class weights exceeding 70B parameters, creating profound systems challenges for consumer hardware. This paper presents a systematic empirical analysis of the Nvidia and Apple Silicon ecosystems, specifically characterizing the distinct intra-architecture trade-offs required to deploy these massive models. On the Nvidia Blackwell architecture, we identify a critical "Backend Dichotomy" within the TensorRT-LLM stack: while the new NVFP4 quantization format delivers a 1.6x throughput advantage over optimized BF16 baselines (151 tokens/s vs. 92 tokens/s), realizing this performance requires navigating complex runtime constraints that trade startup latency for generation speed. Furthermore, we characterize the "VRAM Wall" for 70B+ models: on discrete GPUs, users face a destructive choice between aggressive quantization (e.g., Q2) that degrades model intelligence to fit in VRAM, or PCIe-bottlenecked CPU offloading, which reduces throughput by over 90% compared to full-GPU execution. Conversely, Apple's Unified Memory Architecture (UMA) circumvents these bottlenecks, enabling linear scaling for 80B parameter models at practical 4-bit precisions. This architectural divergence extends to operational sustainability, where Apple's SoC design demonstrates up to a 23x advantage in energy efficiency (tokens/joule). We conclude that for consumer-grade inference, the optimal hardware is defined by a complex interplay between compute density (Nvidia) and memory capacity (Apple), moderated by the significant "ecosystem friction" of proprietary quantization workflows.

ai.one_liner_zh: The paper compares consumer Nvidia Blackwell and Apple Silicon paths for 70B+ local LLM inference, arguing that Nvidia's NVFP4 throughput gains collide with VRAM and runtime friction while Apple UMA offers memory headroom and energy-efficiency advantages.
ai.summary_zh: Local LLM inference is moving toward datacenter-scale 70B+ models, which stresses consumer hardware through memory capacity, bandwidth, runtime setup, and power limits. The paper presents an empirical comparison of Nvidia Blackwell and Apple Silicon, highlighting a TensorRT-LLM backend trade-off where NVFP4 improves throughput over BF16 but introduces runtime constraints and startup-latency trade-offs. It also frames a VRAM wall for large models on discrete GPUs, where aggressive quantization or CPU offload can damage quality or throughput, while Apple UMA is claimed to scale more smoothly for 80B models at practical 4-bit precision. The abstract reports concrete throughput, offload, and energy-efficiency numbers, including a 1.6x NVFP4 throughput gain and up to 23x tokens-per-joule advantage for Apple's SoC design. The main missing detail is the full benchmark setup: model set, prompt lengths, decoding settings, power measurement method, quality impact of quantization, and fairness of software baselines.
ai.background_zh: Consumer local inference is increasingly asked to run 70B+ models, making memory capacity, quantization format, runtime overhead, and power efficiency first-order systems constraints.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks which consumer hardware ecosystem handles large-model inference better when compute density, VRAM limits, unified memory, offloading, and proprietary runtime workflows interact.
ai.approach_zh: It compares Nvidia Blackwell with TensorRT-LLM and Apple Silicon UMA, using measured throughput, energy efficiency, quantization behavior, and offload outcomes to characterize architectural trade-offs.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports empirical measurements, including NVFP4 at 151 tokens/s versus 92 tokens/s for BF16, over 90% throughput loss under CPU offload, linear UMA scaling for 80B models, and up to 23x tokens-per-joule advantage, but it does not specify the full benchmark methodology.
ai.value_zh: If the evaluation is methodologically sound, the paper is useful for choosing consumer inference hardware and for understanding when kernel/runtime optimization is less important than memory architecture and ecosystem friction.
open_questions: What exact models, prompt lengths, batch sizes, decoding settings, and software versions were used for the Nvidia and Apple measurements?
How was quantization quality evaluated, especially the claim that Q2 degrades model intelligence for fitting 70B+ models in VRAM?
How were power and energy-efficiency numbers measured, and were thermal limits, sustained runs, and comparable utilization controlled?
core_contributions: Empirical framing of consumer 70B+ LLM inference as a trade-off between Nvidia compute density and Apple unified-memory capacity.
Identification of a TensorRT-LLM backend trade-off where NVFP4 throughput gains depend on accepting runtime and startup-latency constraints.
Characterization of discrete-GPU VRAM limits, CPU offload penalties, and Apple UMA scaling behavior for large local models.
why_read: Directly matches LLM inference systems and serving-efficiency interests with concrete throughput, memory, and energy signals.
Surfaces practical deployment constraints that are often hidden behind peak tokens-per-second numbers.
Useful as a hardware-selection and runtime-friction case study, provided the full methodology supports the claims.
risks: The abstract gives headline measurements but not enough benchmark detail to judge fairness across hardware and software stacks.
Quantization quality claims may depend strongly on model, task, and evaluation metric, none of which are specified in the abstract.
recommended_for: LLM inference engineers evaluating local 70B+ deployment paths.
Systems researchers studying memory-capacity limits, quantization trade-offs, and runtime overheads on consumer hardware.
Practitioners comparing Nvidia discrete GPUs with Apple Silicon for private or workstation-scale inference.
keywords: NVFP4 quantization provides reported 1.6x throughput over BF16, 151 tokens/s versus 92 tokens/s, while CPU offload is reported to reduce throughput by over 90% and Apple UMA is reported to improve memory scaling and energy efficiency.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit for the configured focus because it is explicitly about LLM inference systems and reports concrete throughput, latency, memory, quantization, and energy-efficiency trade-offs, though the full read must verify benchmark fairness and measurement methodology.
ai.fallback_summary: The operational landscape of local Large Language Model (LLM) inference has shifted from lightweight models to datacenter-class weights exceeding 70B parameters, creating profound systems challenges for consumer hardware.

top_papers.title: VitaLLM: A Versatile and Tiny Accelerator for Mixed-Precision LLM Inference on Edge Devices
top_papers.summary: We present VitaLLM, a mixed precision accelerator that enables ternary weight large language models to run efficiently on edge devices. The design combines two compute cores, a multiplier free TINT core for ternary-INT projections and a BoothFlex core that reuses a radix-4 Booth datapath for both INT8$\times$INT8 attention and ternary-INT-sustaining utilization without duplicating arrays. A predictive sparse attention mechanism employs a leading-one (LO) surrogate with a comparison-free top-$K$ selector to prune key/value (KV) fetches by roughly $1-K/M$ for $M$ cached tokens, confining exact attention to $K$ candidates. System-level integration uses head-level pipelining and an absmax-based quantization barrier to standardize cross-core interfaces and overlap nonlinear reductions with linear tiles. A 16 nm silicon prototype at 1 GHz/0.8 V achieves 72.46 tokens/s in decode and 0.88 s prefill (64 tokens) within 0.214 mm^2 and 120 KB on-chip memory, while reducing KV traffic and improving utilization in ablations. These results demonstrate practical BitNet b1.58 (3B) inference on edge-class platforms and provide a compact blueprint for future mixed-precision LLM accelerators.

ai.one_liner_zh: VitaLLM is a 16 nm mixed-precision edge accelerator for ternary-weight LLM inference that attacks both projection compute and KV-cache traffic with specialized cores and predictive sparse attention.
ai.summary_zh: Edge LLM inference is constrained by decode latency, memory traffic, and mixed-precision support, especially for ternary-weight models that still require attention and KV movement. VitaLLM combines a multiplier-free TINT core for ternary-INT projections with a BoothFlex core that reuses a radix-4 Booth datapath for INT8 attention and ternary-INT utilization, avoiding duplicated compute arrays. It also uses a leading-one surrogate with a comparison-free top-K selector to prune KV fetches by roughly 1-K/M, while head-level pipelining and an absmax quantization barrier coordinate cross-core execution. The abstract reports a 16 nm, 1 GHz/0.8 V prototype reaching 72.46 tokens/s decode and 0.88 s prefill for 64 tokens within 0.214 mm^2 and 120 KB on-chip memory, with ablations claiming lower KV traffic and better utilization. The main missing detail is how broadly these results hold across model sizes, sequence lengths, accuracy impact from sparse attention, and baseline accelerator comparisons.
ai.background_zh: Ternary-weight LLMs reduce model compute and storage, but edge deployment still needs efficient attention, KV-cache access, and mixed-precision datapaths.
ai.problem_zh: The core problem is how to keep a tiny edge accelerator utilized across ternary projections and INT8 attention without letting KV traffic dominate inference latency and energy.
ai.approach_zh: The design pairs specialized mixed-precision cores with predictive sparse attention, head-level pipelining, and a quantization barrier that standardizes data exchange between compute stages.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract provides concrete silicon prototype numbers for decode, prefill, area, clock, voltage, and memory, and says ablations show reduced KV traffic and improved utilization, but it does not specify baseline systems or accuracy impact.
ai.value_zh: If the reported tradeoffs hold, the paper offers a compact hardware blueprint for practical edge-class inference with ternary LLMs and attention-aware memory reduction.
open_questions: What are the accuracy and perplexity effects of the leading-one surrogate and top-K sparse attention across sequence lengths and tasks?
Which baselines are used for decode/prefill, KV traffic, utilization, area, and energy comparisons, and are they normalized fairly?
How sensitive are the gains to BitNet b1.58 3B specifically versus other model sizes, cache lengths, and quantization settings?
core_contributions: A 16 nm mixed-precision accelerator targeting ternary-weight LLM inference on edge devices.
A multiplier-free TINT core for ternary-INT projections and a BoothFlex core reusing a radix-4 Booth datapath for INT8 attention and ternary-INT work.
A predictive sparse attention path that prunes KV fetches by roughly 1-K/M using a leading-one surrogate and comparison-free top-K selection.
why_read: Directly aligned with edge LLM inference acceleration and hardware/runtime co-design.
Includes concrete prototype metrics rather than only architectural simulation claims.
Addresses both compute-array reuse and KV-cache bandwidth, which are central bottlenecks in decode.
risks: The abstract does not report energy efficiency, accuracy degradation, or detailed baseline comparisons.
Sparse attention claims may depend heavily on K, sequence length M, and workload distribution, which are not detailed here.
recommended_for: Hardware architects studying ternary or mixed-precision LLM datapaths
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit for the configured focus because it reports a concrete silicon prototype for LLM inference acceleration, targets decode and prefill bottlenecks, and connects mixed-precision datapath design with KV-traffic reduction.
ai.fallback_summary: We present VitaLLM, a mixed precision accelerator that enables ternary weight large language models to run efficiently on edge devices.

top_papers.title: SAGA: Workflow-Atomic Scheduling for AI Agent Inference on GPU Clusters
top_papers.summary: AI agents execute tens to hundreds of chained LLM calls per task, yet GPU schedulers treat each call as independent, discarding gigabytes of intermediate state between steps and inflating end-to-end latency by 3-8x. We argue that this request-level abstraction is fundamentally mismatched to compound AI workloads, and propose a shift to program-level scheduling: treating the entire agent workflow (not individual inference calls) as the first-class schedulable unit. We present SAGA, a distributed scheduler that implements this abstraction through three mechanisms: (1) Agent Execution Graphs that capture workflow structure to predict KV cache reuse across tool-call boundaries, achieving within 1.31x of Bélády's optimal offline policy; (2) session-affinity batching with work stealing that co-locates correlated requests while maintaining global load balance; and (3) Agent Fair Share, a task-completion-time fairness metric with provable bounded-deviation guarantees. On a 64-GPU cluster serving SWE-bench coding agents and WebArena browser tasks, SAGA reduces task completion time by 1.64x (geometric mean, p < 0.001) over vLLM v0.15.1 with prefix caching and affinity routing, while improving GPU memory utilization by 1.22x and achieving 99.2% SLO attainment under multi-tenant interference. These latency gains come at a quantified cost: approximately 30% lower peak throughput than throughput-optimal batch scheduling, a tradeoff appropriate for the latency-sensitive interactive deployments that dominate compound AI usage. Our results demonstrate that workflow-aware scheduling is essential for efficient compound AI serving.
top_papers.comments: 15 pages, 3 figures, 11 tables. Accepted to HPDC '26 (35th International Symposium on High-Performance Parallel and Distributed Computing), July 13-16, 2026, Cleveland, OH, USA

ai.one_liner_zh: SAGA makes an agent workflow, rather than each LLM call, the scheduling unit for GPU-cluster inference so KV-cache reuse and task-level latency can be optimized across chained calls.
ai.summary_zh: This paper targets compound AI workloads where agents issue many dependent LLM calls and request-level GPU schedulers lose reuse opportunities between steps. SAGA shifts scheduling to the workflow level using Agent Execution Graphs for KV-cache reuse prediction, session-affinity batching with work stealing, and a task-completion-time fairness metric. The abstract reports evaluation on a 64-GPU cluster with SWE-bench coding agents and WebArena browser tasks, claiming 1.64x lower task completion time than vLLM v0.15.1 with prefix caching and affinity routing, plus better memory utilization and SLO attainment. The important systems claim is that latency improves by preserving workflow locality and correlated state, not by a faster kernel or model change. The main caveat is explicit: peak throughput is about 30% lower than throughput-optimal batch scheduling, and the abstract leaves implementation overheads and workload generality for the full paper.
ai.background_zh: Agent serving turns one user task into a chain of correlated LLM calls, which makes per-request scheduling a poor fit when intermediate KV/cache state can carry across steps.
ai.problem_zh: The core question is whether GPU cluster schedulers should optimize task completion time for whole agent workflows instead of maximizing throughput over isolated inference requests.
ai.approach_zh: SAGA represents workflows with Agent Execution Graphs, co-locates related requests through session-affinity batching while using work stealing for balance, and adds Agent Fair Share for task-level fairness.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract supports the claim with a 64-GPU evaluation on SWE-bench and WebArena workloads against a vLLM v0.15.1 baseline with prefix caching and affinity routing, reporting latency, memory-utilization, SLO, and throughput-tradeoff numbers.
ai.value_zh: If the results hold, this is useful for latency-sensitive agent serving systems where preserving workflow locality is more valuable than peak batch throughput.
open_questions: How much scheduler overhead and profiling state does Agent Execution Graph construction add at runtime?
Is the vLLM baseline tuned comparably for batching, prefix caching, affinity routing, and multi-tenant load?
Do the gains persist on less structured or shorter agent workflows where cross-step KV-cache reuse is weaker?
core_contributions: Defines workflow-atomic scheduling as the abstraction for chained agent inference on GPU clusters.
Introduces Agent Execution Graphs to predict KV-cache reuse across tool-call boundaries.
Combines session-affinity batching, work stealing, and a task-completion-time fairness metric for multi-tenant serving.
why_read: Directly matches LLM inference serving and GPU-cluster scheduling concerns.
Gives concrete latency, memory, SLO, and throughput tradeoff claims rather than only proposing an abstraction.
Frames a specific systems tradeoff: lower task latency through workflow locality at the cost of peak throughput.
risks: The reported 30% lower peak throughput may be unacceptable for throughput-dominated batch services.
Results may depend heavily on SWE-bench and WebArena-style agent workflows with reusable intermediate state.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit with LLM inference systems and serving efficiency, with concrete cluster-scale evidence and explicit latency-throughput tradeoff claims that merit a full read.
ai.fallback_summary: AI agents execute tens to hundreds of chained LLM calls per task, yet GPU schedulers treat each call as independent, discarding gigabytes of intermediate state between steps and inflating end-to-end latency by 3-8x.

top_papers.title: LLM-Emu: Native Runtime Emulation of LLM Inference via Profile-Driven Sampling
top_papers.summary: Realistic evaluation of LLM serving systems requires online workloads, dynamic arrivals, queueing, and the serving engine's local scheduling for execution batching, but running such experiments on GPUs is expensive. Existing simulators reduce this cost, but often operate offline or in time-warped mode, re-implement serving-engine schedulers, or require accurate operator/kernel-level latency models. We present LLM-Emu, a serving-native emulator for vLLM that preserves the production HTTP, scheduling, KV-cache, and output-processing paths while replacing only GPU forward execution with profile-sampled latency and synthetic output tokens. Tested on two different GPUs, four model variants, two model families, two attention backends, and both Poisson and bursty ShareGPT workloads, LLM-Emu closely tracks real vLLM serving behavior: TPOT and ITL stay within $4.8\%$ absolute error, E2E latency within $5.3\%$, and output throughput within $1.9\%$; TTFT is less stable, with maximum error $10.4\%$, reflecting its sensitivity to admission and queue state. These results suggest that lightweight, serving-native emulation can support practical online experimentation for LLM-serving systems. LLM-Emu is open sourced at https://github.com/AKafakA/llm-emu.

ai.one_liner_zh: A vLLM-native emulator replaces GPU forward passes with profile-sampled latency while preserving the serving stack needed to approximate online LLM inference behavior cheaply.
ai.summary_zh: Online LLM serving evaluation is expensive because realistic tests need dynamic arrivals, queueing, batching, KV-cache effects, and the serving engine's local scheduler. LLM-Emu keeps vLLM's HTTP, scheduling, KV-cache, and output-processing paths intact, replacing only GPU forward execution with profile-sampled latency and synthetic output tokens. The abstract reports tests across two GPUs, four model variants, two model families, two attention backends, and Poisson plus bursty ShareGPT workloads, with small errors for TPOT, ITL, end-to-end latency, and throughput. TTFT is less stable, which the authors attribute to sensitivity to admission and queue state. The main missing detail for a full read is how profile collection, profile reuse, and workload drift affect accuracy outside the reported setup.
ai.background_zh: LLM serving systems are hard to evaluate cheaply because offline or time-warped simulators often miss production scheduler, queueing, and runtime behavior.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks whether online LLM-serving experiments can be emulated without GPUs while still preserving enough of the real serving stack to predict latency and throughput.
ai.approach_zh: LLM-Emu integrates with vLLM and swaps GPU forward execution for sampled latencies and synthetic tokens while leaving HTTP, scheduling, KV-cache, and output handling in place.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract supports the claim with comparisons against real vLLM on two GPUs, multiple models, attention backends, and workload shapes, reporting low errors for most serving metrics and higher TTFT error.
ai.value_zh: If the reported accuracy holds, this gives systems researchers a cheaper way to test serving policies, load conditions, and runtime behavior before spending GPU time.
open_questions: How are the latency profiles collected, parameterized, and refreshed when hardware, model shape, attention backend, or batch composition changes?
Does preserving vLLM paths remain enough when evaluating scheduler or KV-cache changes that alter queue state and TTFT behavior?
How does the emulator behave on workloads beyond Poisson and bursty ShareGPT traces, especially under saturation or long-context regimes?
core_contributions: Introduces a serving-native emulation path for vLLM rather than a separate offline simulator.
Preserves production HTTP, scheduler, KV-cache, and output-processing paths while emulating GPU forward latency.
Reports accuracy across multiple GPUs, model variants, model families, attention backends, and workload arrival patterns.
why_read: Directly relevant to low-cost evaluation of LLM serving systems and runtime policies.
Useful for understanding which parts of serving behavior require native runtime preservation versus kernel-level simulation.
Provides concrete metric-level error claims that can be checked against real vLLM runs.
risks: TTFT accuracy is explicitly weaker, which matters for admission control and user-visible latency studies.
Profile-sampled latency may not extrapolate to unprofiled hardware, model shapes, attention backends, or kernel changes without additional validation.
recommended_for: LLM serving researchers evaluating scheduling, batching, and queueing behavior.
Systems engineers using vLLM who need cheaper online workload experiments.
Runtime and infrastructure teams comparing simulation, emulation, and GPU-backed serving evaluation methods.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High fit with the LLM inference systems focus: it targets serving evaluation cost, preserves key runtime paths, and reports concrete latency and throughput error metrics, though the full read should verify profile robustness and TTFT limitations.
ai.fallback_summary: Realistic evaluation of LLM serving systems requires online workloads, dynamic arrivals, queueing, and the serving engine's local scheduling for execution batching, but running such experiments on GPUs is expensive.

top_papers.title: Eliminating Hidden Serialization in Multi-Node Megakernel Communication
top_papers.summary: Recent megakernel designs for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) inference fuse expert computation with fine-grained, GPU-initiated communication into a single persistent GPU kernel, and outperform collective-based MoE on a single node by overlapping data transfer with compute at tile granularity. This benefit does not carry over cleanly to multi-node inference, where experts span many nodes connected by an RDMA fabric. Communication-bound MoE models regress by up to $10\times$ on 8 nodes, and the regression worsens with node count. We trace this regression to hidden serialization in proxy-based RDMA transports. The ordering requirement between each tile transfer and its completion signal forces a fence that drains the NIC pipeline, and its cost grows with the number of concurrent transfers. As a result, models whose per-expert compute is too small to absorb this inflated network latency expose communication on the critical path. We present \emph{Perseus}, which eliminates this serialization through two techniques. \emph{Decoupled signaling} batches fences at per-destination granularity, reducing fence count by $8\times$. \emph{NIC-side ordering} replaces proxy stalls with hardware fence flags, so the proxy never blocks. On proxy-based transports, Perseus achieves up to 10.3$\times$ end-to-end speedup. Perseus on IBRC matches or exceeds IBGDA GPU-direct by up to 1.2$\times$, which shows that serialization, rather than the choice between proxy-based and GPU-direct transport, is what bounds multi-node megakernel performance.
top_papers.matched_domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

match_details.domain: LLM Code Generation For CUDA Kernels

ai.one_liner_zh: The paper identifies fence-induced serialization in proxy-based RDMA as the bottleneck that breaks multi-node MoE megakernel scaling and proposes batching and NIC-side ordering to remove it.
ai.summary_zh: Recent MoE megakernels overlap expert compute with fine-grained GPU-initiated communication, but the abstract argues that this single-node advantage collapses across RDMA-connected nodes. The failure mode is hidden serialization in proxy-based transports: each tile transfer must be ordered before its completion signal, forcing fences that drain the NIC pipeline and expose network latency when expert compute is small. Perseus attacks this with decoupled signaling, which batches fences per destination, and NIC-side ordering, which avoids proxy stalls by using hardware fence flags. The abstract reports up to 10.3x end-to-end speedup on proxy-based transports and up to 1.2x over IBGDA GPU-direct, suggesting the bottleneck is ordering serialization rather than proxy transport itself. The missing details are the exact hardware assumptions, workload shapes, and baseline configuration needed to judge how general the result is.
ai.background_zh: MoE inference is increasingly limited by cross-GPU and cross-node communication, especially when persistent megakernels try to overlap small expert-compute tiles with network transfers.
ai.problem_zh: The paper asks why megakernel communication that works on a single node regresses badly at multi-node scale, and whether the bottleneck is transport choice or ordering semantics.
ai.approach_zh: Perseus reduces synchronization pressure through per-destination fence batching and moves ordering enforcement to NIC-side hardware fence flags so the proxy does not block.
ai.evidence_zh: The abstract reports communication-bound MoE regression up to 10x on 8 nodes, an 8x fence-count reduction, up to 10.3x end-to-end speedup on proxy-based transports, and up to 1.2x over IBGDA GPU-direct.
ai.value_zh: If the evaluation holds, this is directly useful for multi-node LLM inference runtimes that rely on GPU-initiated fine-grained communication and need to keep RDMA latency off the critical path.
open_questions: What GPUs, NICs, RDMA transports, node counts, and MoE model shapes were used for the reported speedups?
Are the proxy-based and GPU-direct baselines matched on batching, proxy scheduling, fence semantics, and overlap opportunities?
How portable is NIC-side ordering across current production NICs, and what happens when hardware fence flags are unavailable?
core_contributions: Traces multi-node megakernel slowdown to hidden serialization from per-tile transfer and completion-signal ordering.
Introduces decoupled signaling that batches fences per destination and reduces fence count by 8x.
Introduces NIC-side ordering to avoid proxy stalls while preserving required transfer-signal ordering.
why_read: Strong fit for LLM inference systems and low-level communication/runtime co-design.
Gives a concrete mechanism for why GPU-initiated megakernels can lose their overlap advantage at RDMA scale.
Targets a specific kernel/runtime bottleneck rather than presenting generic MoE serving optimizations.
risks: The abstract does not specify hardware details, so the NIC-side ordering requirement may limit deployability.

The reported wins may depend on communication-bound MoE shapes with too little expert compute to hide inflated network latency absolute behavior for compute-heavy models is unclear from the abstract alone may be smaller for compute-heavy models than for the highlighted cases simply not addressed by the abstract.
recommended_for: LLM serving engineers working on multi-node MoE inference.
Runtime and communication-stack researchers optimizing RDMA-backed GPU communication.
CUDA megakernel and persistent-kernel developers concerned with overlap, fences, and proxy transports.
ai.reading_priority_reason: High priority because it directly matches multi-node LLM inference acceleration and provides a concrete bottleneck mechanism with reported end-to-end speedups, though full confidence depends on hardware and baseline details not included in the abstract.
ai.fallback_summary: Recent megakernel designs for Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) inference fuse expert computation with fine-grained, GPU-initiated communication into a single persistent GPU kernel, and outperform collective-based MoE on a single node by overlapping data transfer with compute at tile granularity.

daily_brief.overview_zh: Today's main threads are training and inference efficiency, factual reliability and verification. The set is anchored by Making Every Verified Token Count:… and Silicon Showdown: Performance,…. VitaLLM: A Versatile and Tiny… adds another data point on whether the same line of work carries stronger evidence or engineering value.
reading_strategy: Start with Making Every Verified Token Count:… and verify whether it defines the factual reliability and verification problem clearly and exposes a real mechanism.
Then read Silicon Showdown: Performance,… to see whether the novelty comes from model design, training procedure, or evaluation reframing.
Use VitaLLM: A Versatile and Tiny… as a final cross-check on experimental boundaries and practical value.
